QrCode.title: QrCode
QrCode.description: Represents a generated QR code, including its original source URL, the URL to the generated image, and associated metadata.

id.description: Unique identifier for the QrCode entity.

originalUrl.description: The original URL or text content that was encoded into this QR code.

qrCodeImageUrl.description: The URL where the generated QR code image (e.g., PNG, JPG, SVG) is stored, typically in a cloud storage service like Firebase Storage.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the QR code was generated and saved.

status.description: The current operational status of the QR code (e.g., 'active', 'inactive'). Inactive QR codes might not be displayed or functional.

userId.description: Reference to the User who generated or saved this QR code. (Relationship: User 1:N QrCode)

definition.description: Stores core user metadata. This collection acts as the parent for all user-specific data, including generated QR codes.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication UID of the user.

definition.description: Stores individual QR codes generated by a specific user. Includes denormalized 'userId' (matching path segment) for robust authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication UID of the user who owns this QR code.

params.name: qrCodeId
params.description: The unique identifier for the specific QR code document.

definition.description: This collection identifies users with administrative privileges. The existence of a document where the ID matches a user's UID grants them admin status for database access control.

params.name: adminId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication UID of the user designated as an administrator.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to be secure, scalable, and highly debuggable, adhering strictly to the core design principles. User-specific QR codes are stored in subcollections under their respective user documents at `/users/{userId}/qr_codes/{qrCodeId}`. This hierarchical path-based ownership is the cornerstone for achieving **Authorization Independence**. Each `QrCode` document explicitly includes a `userId` field, which directly corresponds to the `{userId}` wildcard in its path. This denormalization means that security rules can evaluate access requests by comparing `request.auth.uid` directly with `{userId}` from the path, or the `userId` field within the document, without needing any expensive or non-atomic `get()` operations on parent documents. This structure also inherently supports atomic operations for creating or updating a QR code.

**Querying and Authorization Predicates (QAPs)** are effectively supported for both regular users and administrators. For regular users, listing their own QR codes is straightforward and secure: they query `db.collection('users').doc(request.auth.uid).collection('qr_codes')`. Firestore security rules can then easily ensure that `request.auth.uid` matches the `{userId}` in the path, preventing users from accessing or listing others' QR codes. This fulfills the QAP principle by making security predicates an inherent part of the query path, rather than requiring post-query filtering. For the admin dashboard, which requires global oversight, a **Collection Group Query** on `qr_codes` can be utilized. This allows an administrator to efficiently retrieve all QR codes across all users. Admin privileges are managed via a dedicated `/roles_admin/{adminId}` collection (DBAC principle), where the mere existence of a document signifies admin status. Firestore rules can check for `exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/roles_admin/$(request.auth.uid))` to grant `list` access on the `qr_codes` collection group, ensuring admins can view all QR codes without violating QAPs.

**Structural Segregation** is maintained by separating user-owned data (`/users/{userId}/qr_codes`) from global roles (`/roles_admin`). All documents within a `/users/{userId}/qr_codes` subcollection share the same ownership-based security posture, simplifying rules. The `status` field within the `QrCode` entity provides explicit state modeling, enhancing data clarity and predictability. Consistent naming (`userId`, `qrCodeId`, `adminId`) ensures predictability and debuggability across the database.
